summary: SSH worked; I read the full decompiled C# tree (GamingCenter, Tray, DefaultRGBKB) plus the local RE artifacts. Key result: the old OEM Gaming Center does NOT use the Windows power subsystem at all — there is zero use of powrprof.dll, PowerSetActiveScheme, PowerWrite*ValueIndex, overlay schemes, or any power GUID (the only GUIDs are GUID_DEVCLASS_BATTERY for battery enumeration and installer product GUIDs). "Power modes" (Gaming/Office/ECO/Standard) and fan modes are implemented entirely by writing bytes into EC RAM through a WMI ACPI test interface (AcpiTest_MULong.GetSetULong on ACPI\PNP0C14\1_1). Crucially there IS an observable push signal: the app subscribes to the WMI event class AcpiTest_EventULong (root\WMI) which fires with a ULong scancode whenever the EC/hardware raises an event — including the physical fan-mode Q-key (0xB0), fan-boost (0xA7), and battery/thermal events. Additionally the app mirrors the committed fan mode into HKLM\SOFTWARE\OEM\GamingCenter\Monitor\FanMode (mode: 0=gaming, 2=office) and the Tray watches it with a RegNotifyChangeKeyValue-based RegistryMonitor. AC/DC transitions are detected via .NET SystemEvents.PowerModeChanged + SystemInformation.PowerStatus. There is no named pipe and no dedicated power service; OemServiceWinApp.dll (OemSvcHook) is an alternate hardware-access path used for RGB/lightbar, not power. Our app can hook the exact same three signals to stay in sync.

patterns.name: EC read/write via WMI ACPI test interface (AcpiTest_MULong.GetSetULong)
patterns.whatItIs: All EC access goes through System.Management: new ManagementObject("root\WMI", "AcpiTest_MULong.InstanceName='ACPI\PNP0C14\1_1'").InvokeMethod("GetSetULong", {Data}). READ encodes Data = 0x10000000000 (1099511627776) + Addr and returns ["Return"]; WRITE encodes Data = (Value << 16) + Addr. A second object AcpiODM_Demo (ACPI\PNP0C14\2_0) method GetUlongEx7 is used for BIOS-ROM writes. Evidence: GamingCenter/Utility/WMIEC.cs (WMIReadECRAM/WMIWriteECRAM), GamingCenter/GamingCenter/WMIEC.cs, Tray/GamingCenterTray/WMIEC.cs.
patterns.fitForUs: Directly reusable and already validated: analysis/ec-read-probe.json (produced by read-ec-probe.ps1) shows these exact addresses read successfully (e.g. 0x751=fan ctl, 0x741, 0x782). This is the authoritative source-of-truth for live state; our app can poll it with the identical addr+0x10000000000 read encoding.
patterns.tradeoffs: Poll-only (no push); each call spins up a WMI/ManagementObject invoke so keep the poll interval modest (old app polls sensors every 2000ms via DispatcherTimer). Requires admin/elevated WMI ACPI access. Writing needs the same shift-encoding or you corrupt the address.

patterns.name: WMI event push: ManagementEventWatcher on AcpiTest_EventULong
patterns.whatItIs: At static ctor time WMIEC starts a ManagementEventWatcher over WqlEventQuery("SELECT * FROM AcpiTest_EventULong") in scope \\.\Root\WMI. Each event carries SystemProperties["ULong"] = a scancode. The handler switches on it: 165 WinKey, 57/58/175 LightBar, 167 (0xA7) fan-boost update, 170/173 over/under-temp, 172 MyBat AC, 174 battery alert, 176 (0xB0) QKey_FanModeSwitch (toggles Gaming/Office). Evidence: GamingCenter/GamingCenter/WMIEC.cs StartWMIReceiveEvent + WMIHandleEvent; scancode constants in ECSpec.cs (QKey_FanModeSwitch=176, OSD_FANBOOST_UPDATE=167).
patterns.fitForUs: This is THE observable hook to react to changes made outside our app. When the user presses the physical fan/mode key, the EC broadcasts this WMI event system-wide; multiple subscribers can receive it. Our app should run its own ManagementEventWatcher on the same query and, on any relevant scancode (esp. 176/167), re-read the EC to refresh mode/PL. Works even if the old app is closed, because it's the EC/BIOS that raises it.
patterns.tradeoffs: It signals 'something happened', not the new value — you must follow up with an EC read to learn the resulting mode. Event delivery depends on the OEM WMI ACPI driver being present. Payload is a single ULong scancode, board-specific meaning.

patterns.name: Registry mode mirror + RegistryMonitor (RegNotifyChangeKeyValue)
patterns.whatItIs: The main app writes the committed fan/power mode to HKLM\SOFTWARE\OEM\GamingCenter\Monitor\FanMode value 'mode' (DWORD 0=gaming, 2=office) via SetRegistry_TrayMonitor. The Tray subscribes with a RegistryMonitor (a RegNotifyChangeKeyValue wrapper from an external RegistryUtils namespace) and updates its icon in OnRegChanged. Full persisted state lives under HKLM\SOFTWARE\OEM\GamingCenter\MyFan3\ (FanMode, OfficeMode, OfficeAdvancedMode, OfficeBasicLevel, LevelTemp1-5, FanModeAutoSave) and \MyFan3\GamingModePL / \OfficeModePL (Default_PL1/2/4). Evidence: FanManagementPage2.cs:1952 SetRegistry_TrayMonitor, LoadRegistry lines 229-286; Tray/GamingCenterTray/MyFan.cs StartMonitor/OnRegChanged; Tray MainWindow.cs also RegistryMonitors HKLM\SOFTWARE\OEM\GamingCenter for language.
patterns.fitForUs: Clean, cheap push signal we can watch with RegNotifyChangeKeyValue (or ManagementEventWatcher on RegistryValueChangeEvent). Watching Monitor\FanMode\mode tells us instantly when the old app commits a Gaming<->Office switch, and MyFan3\* gives us the full intended config without EC decoding.
patterns.tradeoffs: Only written while the OLD app is running. A hardware-key change made with the old app closed updates the EC + fires the WMI event but does NOT update this registry key — so registry is a convenience/fast-path, not a complete substitute for EC polling. Values are HKLM (needs admin to write; read is fine).

patterns.name: Power modes are EC PL bytes, NOT Windows power plans
patterns.whatItIs: Gaming/Office/ECO/Standard map to PL1/PL2/PL4 + Tau values written to EC RAM: SetPL124Tau -> WMIWriteECRAM(1923=0x783 PL1, 1924=0x784 PL2, 1925=0x785 PL4) plus Tau. MyFan3 logic (FanManagementPage2.cs SetFanMode): Gaming AC = GamingMode_PL* defaults, Gaming DC = 0,0,0; Office Basic AC = OfficeMode defaults; Office Advanced ECO AC = 25/25/PL4def; Office Advanced Standard AC = Office defaults; all DC branches = 0,0,0. Defaults are read from EC (Gaming PL 1840-1842=0x730+, Office PL 1844-1846) and cached in registry. IntelTunningID.cs defines Intel XTU tuning IDs (PL1=48, PL2=47, PL1_TAU=66, PL2_TAU=67) but the active write path in this build is EC RAM, not XTU/powrprof. Confirmed no powrprof/PowerSetActiveScheme/overlay/GUID_* anywhere in the tree.
patterns.fitForUs: Means our app must NOT try to read Windows power-plan/overlay GUIDs to learn the OEM 'power mode' — that state does not live there. To mirror it, read EC PL bytes (0x783/0x784/0x785) and/or the MyFan3 registry, and combine with fan control byte + AC/DC. If our app also wants to SET modes compatibly, write the same PL/Tau bytes (respecting AC vs DC branch).
patterns.tradeoffs: PL semantics are AC/DC-dependent and board-specific; DC forces 0/0/0 (EC/BIOS default cap). Values are raw watts in EC units. Overwriting must mirror the old app's AC/DC branching or you get inconsistent caps.

patterns.name: Fan-mode control byte mapping at EC 0x751 (1873)
patterns.whatItIs: ECSpec.MyFanCTLByteFlag defines the control byte written to ADDR_MAFAN_CONTROL_BYTE=1873 (0x751): Normal=0x00, FanBoost=0x40, User_Fan_Mode=0x80, User_Fan_Level1-5=0x81-0x85, User_Fan_HiMode=0xA0. MyFan2 SetFanMode writes 0x00 (smart/gaming), 0x40 (boost), 0x80|level (customize); MyFan3 uses 0x00 gaming, 0xA0 office-advanced, 0x80+level basic, +0x40 fan-enhanced. Boost detection reads (byte & 0xC0)&0x40. Capability/rule flags come from BIOS_OEM_BYTE2=1922 (0x782): bit0 new MyFan2 rule, bit1 Q-key, bit4 default mode gaming/office. Evidence: ECSpec.cs enum + consts; FanManagementPage.cs:837 SetFanMode; FanManagementPage2.cs:572 SetFanMode/SetBasicFanLevel; MyFan.cs reads 0x782.
patterns.fitForUs: Gives us the exact byte-level decode to read current fan mode straight from EC 0x751 and capability bits from 0x782 — the reliable cross-app truth. ec-read-probe.json already confirms 0x751 and 0x782 are readable on this machine.
patterns.tradeoffs: Two different rule sets (MyFan2 vs MyFan3) select via 0x782 bit0 and a SupportMyFan3 flag; our decoder must branch on the same capability bits. Speed PWM tables (0x743-0x747) are separate addresses.

patterns.name: AC/DC detection via SystemEvents.PowerModeChanged (standard .NET)
patterns.whatItIs: MainWindow subscribes SystemEvents.PowerModeChanged; on PowerModes.StatusChange it calls GetACLineStatus() (SystemInformation.PowerStatus.PowerLineStatus Online/Offline -> g_ACLineStatus) then FanManagementPage2.PowerModeChanged(status) to re-apply PL for the new AC/DC state. Evidence: GamingCenter/GamingCenter/MainWindow.cs:186,257-281,2331; FanManagementPage2.cs:473 PowerModeChanged, PowerMode field.
patterns.fitForUs: Standard Windows mechanism our WPF app can use verbatim (Microsoft.Win32.SystemEvents.PowerModeChanged). Needed because the OEM PL values differ by AC vs DC, so to correctly interpret/mirror the mode we must also track power source. No OEM-specific dependency.
patterns.tradeoffs: StatusChange also fires for battery-percentage ticks, so debounce and read PowerLineStatus rather than assuming a transition.

recommendation: To keep OUR WPF .NET 10 app in sync with mode/fan/PL changes made in the old OEM app (or by the hardware Q-key), watch three signals and treat the EC as source of truth: (1) Subscribe to the WMI event ManagementEventWatcher(new WqlEventQuery(\"SELECT * FROM AcpiTest_EventULong\"), scope root\\WMI) and, on any scancode (especially 176 QKey_FanModeSwitch and 167 fan-boost, plus 172/173/174 battery/thermal), re-read the EC — this is the only real push signal that fires even when the old app is closed. (2) Add a RegNotifyChangeKeyValue / RegistryValueChangeEvent watch on HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\OEM\\GamingCenter\\Monitor\\FanMode (mode: 0=gaming,2=office) and on HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\OEM\\GamingCenter\\MyFan3\\* for the fast-path when the old app is running. (3) Subscribe to SystemEvents.PowerModeChanged to track AC/DC because PL interpretation depends on it. On any of these, poll the EC via AcpiTest_MULong.GetSetULong (read encoding addr+0x10000000000): fan-control byte 0x751/1873 (decode with MyFanCTLByteFlag), capability byte 0x782/1922, and PL settings 0x783/0x784/0x785 (1923-1925). Do NOT look at Windows power plans/overlay schemes — the OEM power mode is not stored there. Keep EC polling as the authoritative reconciliation (e.g. ~1-2s like the old app's DispatcherTimer) with the WMI event + registry watch as low-latency triggers. Reuse the existing analysis/read-ec-probe.ps1 encoding, already proven in ec-read-probe.json.
